prompt_queue.prompt: Bootstrap the repository's non-technical product documentation for frontend users. Treat this as a first-run user documentation bootstrap. Study README overview language, existing user docs, UI strings and visible labels, product assets, support-style issues, release notes, and pull requests that describe user-visible behavior. Begin by building a private inventory of product features, user journeys, onboarding paths, settings users see, permissions or requirements users encounter, common tasks, expected outcomes, compatibility boundaries, troubleshooting paths, FAQ-worthy behaviors, and the existing user documentation information architecture. Use that inventory to create a clean documentation surface that helps a new user understand the product and complete common tasks confidently. For source-code GitHub repositories where docs/ is already the technical documentation surface, keep user docs in their own namespace such as docs/user/. Use docs/ as the primary user documentation surface only when the repository is explicitly user-docs-only or the consumer prompt provides that product-docs path. Choose a written scope that fits the run budget, then place additional inventory items in a future coverage section as plain text with reasons. Organize the output into a concise user docs index plus separate digestible task/topic pages whose hierarchy mirrors the product's user-facing architecture, feature areas, and natural task flows. Use clear titles, bounded scope, parent/child relationships, and useful cross-links so readers can move from overview to focused instructions without reading a monolithic page. Include practical product details: prerequisites as users experience them, setup steps through the product surface, expected outcomes, permission requirements, troubleshooting cues, and behavior notes. Write topic pages first, then write or update the user docs index last so navigation reflects written files. Make the index describe the written documentation scope and link only to written pages. Write every linked documentation page before finishing. Use the runner-provided workspace handle for all repository file changes. After writing docs, run workspace_git_status and workspace_git_diff so the runner can commit, push, and open the pull request from the remaining workspace changes. Reconcile the written docs against the private product inventory before finishing and include future coverage with the reason each deferred area remains out of scope.
